The people of Shechem chose Abimelech as their king. They did not ask God whether they should have a king, let alone who it should be. If parents could see what their children would do and what they will suffer, their joy in them often would turn into sorrow; we may be thankful that we cannot know what will happen. Above all, we should fear and watch against sin; for our bad behavior may produce serious effects on our families even after we are gone.

The trees had no reason to choose a king, because they are all the Lord’s trees which he has planted. Nor did Israel have any occasion to set a king over them, for the Lord was their King. Those who produce good results for the public good are justly respected and honored by everyone who is wise, more than those who merely appear important. All these fruit trees gave about the same reason for their refusal to be promoted over the trees; or, as the margin reads it, to go up and down among the trees. To rule involves a great deal of both toil and care. Those who are chosen for public trust and power must give up all private interests and advantages for the good of others. And those advanced to honor and dignity are in great danger of losing their fruitfulness. For this reason, those who want to do good are afraid of becoming too powerful. Jotham compares Abimelech to a bramble or thistle, a worthless plant whose end is to be burned. That is what Abimelech was like.

Abimelech is seated on the throne his father refused. But how long will this glory last? Just three years, and you will see the bramble withered and burned. The prosperity of the wicked is short and unreliable. The people of Shechem are plagued by no one other than Abimelech. They raised him unjustly to the throne; they first feel the weight of his rule.

Abimelech intended to punish the people of Shechem for rejecting him, but God punished them for serving him in the murder of Gideon's sons. When God uses people as instruments to do his work, he means one thing, and they mean another. What they hoped would be for their welfare proved a trap, as those will certainly find who run to idols for shelter; such will prove a refuge of lies.

The people of Shechem were ruined by Abimelech; now he is counted with them, who was their leader in wickedness. Evil pursues sinners and sometimes overtakes them, when they are not only comfortable but triumphant. Though wickedness may prosper for a while, it will not prosper always. The history of mankind, if truly told, would greatly resemble that of this chapter. The records of what are called splendid events present to us such contests for power. Such scenes, though praised by people, fully explain the Scripture teaching about the deceitfulness and desperate wickedness of the human heart, the force of people’s desires, and the effect of Satan's influence. Lord, you have given us your word of truth and righteousness; O pour upon us your spirit of purity, peace, and love, and write your holy law in our hearts.
